original_abstract:
This paper presents a comprehensive empirical evaluation of ChatGPT’s performance as a general-purpose natural language processing (NLP) task solver. While large language models (LLMs) have previously demonstrated strong zero-shot capabilities, the specific generalist potential of ChatGPT across a broad range of tasks remained largely unquantified. To address this, the authors evaluate ChatGPT on 20 benchmark datasets spanning seven core NLP categories: reasoning, natural language inference, question answering, dialogue, summarization, named entity recognition, and sentiment analysis. 

The study reveals that while ChatGPT serves as a capable generalist, its performance varies significantly by task type. It demonstrates remarkable proficiency in arithmetic reasoning and dialogue-based tasks, often outperforming its predecessor, GPT-3.5. However, it still falls short of task-specific fine-tuned models in most scenarios. The findings indicate that ChatGPT struggles with specific structured tasks like sequence tagging and tends to produce overly verbose summaries that can negatively impact standard ROUGE metrics. Through quantitative analysis and qualitative case studies, the authors provide a detailed profile of the model's strengths—such as its preference for factual consistency—and its remaining limitations, offering a foundational reference for the future development and application of general-purpose LLMs.
humanized_abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have been extensively applied to a wide variety of natural language processing (NLP) tasks in recent years, and most previous works show that many large language models exhibit strong zero-shot performance. In this work, we provide an in-depth empirical analysis on the generalist Capabilities of a general-purpose conversational language model, ChatGPT. Its potential for use as a general-purpose NLP task solver remains almost unexplored. In this paper, we investigate the ability of ChatGPT to perform NLP tasks and measure its performance on 20 benchmark datasets across seven common NLP tasks: reasoning, natural language inference, question answering, dialogue, summarization, named entity recognition and sentiment analysis.

Our study investigates the capabilities and limitations of the recently released ChatGPT conversational language model. We find that ChatGPT behaves as a versatile general-purpose language model, with performance differing substantially across diverse sets of tasks. While ChatGPT proves to be highly effective in basic arithmetic computations and conversational dialogues, it is generally outperformed by task-specific fine-tuned models, and only occasionally matches the performance of the parent model, GPT-3.5. For a number of tasks, such as sequence tagging, ChatGPT’s performance is poor, and, at the same time, its output is often too long and tends to produce over-explaining summaries, which can harm the standard ROUGE metric. Our study employs a combination of empirical evaluation and case studies, shedding light on the model’s preferences, behaviors and its limitations, including factual consistency, avoiding unreasonable explanations, avoiding output that is too long, and avoiding references to unverifiable sources. We provide a comprehensive characterization of the ChatGPT model, which can serve as a benchmark for future work in developing general-purpose large language models.

document.output:

This paper presents a comprehensive empirical evaluation of ChatGPT’s performance as a general-purpose natural language processing (NLP) task solver.
